section.title: Paul before Festus and the Appeal to Caesar
section.sectionRationale: A new governor inherits Paul's case, and the old plot revives. Pressed to be handed back to Jerusalem, Paul plays the one card a Roman citizen holds and appeals to Caesar. In a single sentence the destination of the whole book is fixed: to Caesar you will go. Festus then stages a hearing before King Agrippa to make sense of a charge he cannot understand, a dispute about a dead man whom Paul says is alive.
section.thesis: When justice bends to political favor, Paul appeals to Caesar and seals his road to Rome; and a baffled governor confesses that the whole case comes down to one thing, a certain Jesus who had died, but whom Paul affirms to be alive.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Festus faces the renewed plot against Paul; Paul appeals to Caesar and his course to Rome is set; Festus lays the puzzling case before King Agrippa; and Paul is brought with pomp before the assembled court.

verses.text: Three days after his arrival in the province, Festus went up from Caesarea to Jerusalem,
where the chief priests and Jewish leaders presented their case against Paul. They urged Festus
to grant them a concession against Paul by summoning him to Jerusalem, because they were preparing an ambush to kill him along the way.
But Festus replied, “Paul is being held in Caesarea, and I myself am going there soon.
So if this man has done anything wrong, let some of your leaders come down with me and accuse him there.”
After spending no more than eight or ten days with them, Festus went down to Caesarea. The next day he sat on the judgment seat and ordered that Paul be brought in.
When Paul arrived, the Jews who had come down from Jerusalem stood around him, bringing many serious charges that they could not prove.
Then Paul made his defense: “I have committed no offense against the law of the Jews or against the temple or against Caesar.”
But Festus, wishing to do the Jews a favor, said to Paul, “Are you willing to go up to Jerusalem to stand trial before me on these charges?”
Paul replied, “I am standing before the {{bema|judgment seat}} of Caesar, where I ought to be tried. I have done nothing wrong to the Jews, as you yourself know very well.

If, however, I am guilty of anything worthy of death, I do not refuse to die. But if there is no truth to their accusations against me, no one has the right to hand me over to them. I {{epikaloumai|appeal}} to Caesar!”
Then Festus conferred with his council and replied, “You have appealed to Caesar. To Caesar you will go!”

units.label: Festus lays the case before Agrippa
units.note: a dispute about a dead man said to be alive

verses.text: After several days had passed, King Agrippa and Bernice came down to Caesarea to pay their respects to Festus.
Since they were staying several days, Festus laid out Paul’s case before the king: “There is a certain man whom Felix left in prison.
While I was in Jerusalem, the chief priests and elders of the Jews presented their case and requested a judgment against him.
I told them that it is not the Roman custom to hand a man over before he has had an opportunity to face his accusers and defend himself against their charges.
So when they came here with me, I did not delay. The next day I sat on the judgment seat and ordered that the man be brought in.
But when his accusers rose to speak, they did not charge him with any of the crimes I had expected.
They only had some contentions with him regarding their own religion and a certain Jesus who had died, but whom Paul affirmed to be {{zao|alive}}.
Since I was at a loss as to how to investigate these matters, I asked if he was willing to go to Jerusalem and be tried there on these charges.
But when Paul appealed to be held over for the decision of the {{sebastos|Emperor}}, I ordered that he be held until I could send him to Caesar.”
Then Agrippa said to Festus, “I would like to hear this man myself.” “Tomorrow you will hear him,” Festus declared.

units.label: The prisoner presented with pomp
units.note: no charge to write against him

verses.text: The next day Agrippa and Bernice came with great pomp and entered the auditorium, along with the commanders and leading men of the city. And Festus ordered that Paul be brought in.

Then Festus said, “King Agrippa and all who are present with us, you see this man. The whole Jewish community has petitioned me about him, both here and in Jerusalem, crying out that he ought not to live any longer.
But I found he had done nothing worthy of death, and since he has now appealed to the Emperor, I decided to send him.

I have nothing definite to write to our sovereign about him. Therefore I have brought him before all of you, and especially before you, King Agrippa, so that after this inquiry I may have something to write.
For it seems unreasonable to me to send on a prisoner without specifying the charges against him.”

bema.gloss: A raised platform or tribunal, the seat from which a magistrate gave judgment. Festus sits on the bema, and Paul says he is already standing before the bema of Caesar. The word carries a deeper echo in the New Testament, where all must appear before the judgment seat, the bema, of God, Romans 14:10.

epikaloumai.gloss: To call upon, to invoke, and in legal use to appeal to a higher court. As a Roman citizen Paul formally appeals to Caesar, taking the case out of provincial hands. The same verb is used for calling on the name of the Lord to be saved; Paul the citizen appeals to Caesar, but his hope rests in a higher throne.

zao.gloss: To live, to be alive. Festus reduces the whole controversy to one baffling point: a certain Jesus who had died, whom Paul kept insisting was alive. Without knowing it, the puzzled governor names the very heart of the gospel, the resurrection of the crucified Jesus.

sebastos.gloss: The revered or august one, the Greek rendering of the Latin title Augustus given to the Roman emperor. Paul appeals to the Sebastos, the highest earthly authority; yet the book has already shown that Jesus is Lord over Caesar, and the gospel is bound for Rome to declare it.

basic.context: Two years after Felix left him in prison, Paul faces a new governor, Festus. The Jerusalem leaders immediately press to have Paul sent up to them, planning another ambush. Festus refuses to move the venue that far but offers a compromise. To escape being handed back, Paul exercises his right as a Roman citizen and appeals to Caesar, which settles his journey to Rome. Festus then consults the visiting King Agrippa about how to frame the charges.
basic.hermeneutics: Follow the legal maneuvering. The Jews want the trial moved to Jerusalem; Festus wants to please them without breaking Roman custom; Paul refuses the trap and appeals to Caesar. The climax is the appeal in verses 10 to 12. Then watch how Festus, in explaining the case to Agrippa, unwittingly states the gospel: the dispute is about a dead man Paul says is alive.
basic.originalLanguages: Festus sits on the bema, the judgment seat, and Paul says he already stands before Caesar's bema. Paul makes the formal appeal, epikaloumai, that only a citizen could make. Festus sums up the whole matter as a question about whether Jesus is zao, alive. And Paul appeals to the sebastos, the august emperor, though a greater Lord is really in charge.
basic.typology: Paul, innocent yet handed toward Rome by the appeal, walks a road patterned on his Lord, who was also declared innocent by his judges yet delivered up. The Roman insistence that a prisoner face his accusers, and the repeated verdict of no wrong, echo the innocence of Christ before Pilate.
basic.christ: Festus cannot see it, but the case turns entirely on Jesus. The one point he can identify is a certain Jesus who had died, whom Paul affirmed to be alive. That is the resurrection, the pivot of the gospel. Every court in these chapters keeps circling the empty tomb, because the risen Christ is the reason Paul stands there at all.
basic.god: God is steering the story. The plot to kill Paul fails; the appeal to Caesar succeeds; and the promise that Paul would testify in Rome moves toward fulfillment. Human schemes and imperial procedures alike serve the purpose of God to carry His witness to the heart of the empire.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 23:11 promised Paul would testify in Rome. Acts 9:15 called him to carry Christ's name before kings. Romans 14:10 speaks of standing before the judgment seat of God. Philippians 1:12 to 13 shows Paul's chains advancing the gospel even in Rome.
basic.oneStory: The ascended Christ is bringing His gospel to the ends of the earth, and here the road to Rome is locked in. An appeal that looks like a legal tactic is in fact the hand of God, carrying His servant, and His witness to the risen Jesus, straight to Caesar's court.

context.literary: This is the second of the three closing trials, bridging Felix and the grand hearing before Agrippa in chapter 26. Luke uses Festus's report to Agrippa to summarize the case for the reader and to sharpen its true center: not sedition, but the resurrection of Jesus. The appeal to Caesar functions as the narrative hinge that turns the whole book toward Rome.
context.historical: Porcius Festus succeeded Felix around AD 59 and was, by contrast, a more competent and fair-minded governor, though still eager to please the provincials. King Agrippa the second was the last of the Herods, ruler of a small kingdom and overseer of the temple, with expert knowledge of Jewish affairs. Bernice was his sister. A Roman citizen's appeal to the emperor removed a case from provincial jurisdiction to Rome.
context.canonical: The appeal to Caesar fulfills the risen Lord's word that Paul must bear witness in Rome, Acts 23:11, and the commission to testify before kings, Acts 9:15. Festus's summary, a dead Jesus whom Paul says is alive, distills the resurrection message that runs through every sermon in Acts.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God directing Paul to Rome through his appeal to Caesar, to underline again that Paul has done nothing worthy of death, and to expose the true heart of the controversy as the resurrection of Jesus.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Paul's use of his legal rights and the workings of Roman justice; it does not command believers to make the same appeals. The abiding truths are God's providential rule over events, the innocence of the gospel, and the centrality of the risen Christ.

debates.question: Was Paul right to appeal to Caesar rather than trust God more directly?

views.label: A wise and faithful use of lawful rights
views.case: Paul had a promise that he would reach Rome, Acts 23:11, and the appeal was the God-given means; using his citizenship to escape an unjust venue is presented positively, and the Lord later confirms the outcome by an angel, Acts 27:24.

views.label: A merely pragmatic escape with mixed results
views.case: Some note Agrippa's remark that Paul could have been freed had he not appealed, Acts 26:32, and wonder whether the appeal prolonged his imprisonment; on this reading it was a lawful but costly choice rather than a clear command.

debates.bearing: Both agree God used the appeal to fulfill His promise. The passage encourages trusting God's sovereignty while acting responsibly, and warns against pitting faith against the wise use of lawful means; whichever way the appeal is read, the Lord's purpose to bring Paul to Rome stands.

study.originalLanguages: The legal drama is carried by its vocabulary. Festus takes his seat on the bema, the judgment seat, and Paul answers that he already stands before Caesar's bema, where he ought to be tried. He then makes the formal appeal, epikaloumai, the citizen's right to invoke a higher court. Festus, reporting to Agrippa, cannot escape the real question, whether Jesus is zao, alive, after dying. And Paul appeals to the sebastos, the august emperor, though Acts everywhere insists that the true Lord stands above Caesar.

typology.type: The innocent prisoner delivered toward Rome
typology.fulfillment: Patterned on Christ, declared innocent yet handed over, Luke 23:14 to 15; Paul follows his Lord's path of unjust delivery turned to God's purpose.
The earthly tribunal points to the final bema of God, before which all will stand, Romans 14:10 and 2 Corinthians 5:10.

typology.type: A dead Jesus said to be alive
typology.fulfillment: The stumbling point for Festus is the very center of the faith, the bodily resurrection of the crucified Christ, 1 Corinthians 15:3 to 4.

study.christ: Though Festus speaks of Him as a mere puzzle, Jesus is the whole matter. The governor can name only one fixed point in the dispute: a certain Jesus who had died, whom Paul kept affirming to be alive. That is the gospel in a sentence. The risen Christ is why Paul is on trial, why he appeals to Caesar, and why he will preach in Rome. Every court fails to grasp it, yet cannot get past it: the crucified one lives.
study.god: God's sovereign hand governs procedure and politics alike. He foils the ambush, guides the appeal, and moves His servant toward the capital exactly as He promised. Even a governor's desire to please the crowd and a citizen's legal right become instruments of the divine plan. The God who raised Jesus now steers His witness to the throne of the empire to testify that Jesus lives.

crossReferences.note: The Lord's promise that Paul must testify in Rome, now advanced by the appeal to Caesar.
Paul chosen to carry Christ's name before Gentiles and kings, fulfilled as he stands before governors and Agrippa.
Agrippa's later verdict that Paul could have been released had he not appealed to Caesar.
All will stand before the judgment seat, the bema, of God, the deeper reality behind every earthly tribunal.

study.oneStory: From Jerusalem the gospel presses outward to the ends of the earth, and now to Rome itself. Paul's appeal to Caesar, driven by hostility and granted by law, is really the providence of God carrying His witness to the risen Jesus into the emperor's court. The one question every judge keeps circling, is Jesus alive, is the question on which the whole world turns.

quiz.q: Why did Paul appeal to Caesar?

options.text: To avoid being handed back to Jerusalem, where he would face an unjust trial and a plot.
options.note: Festus proposed moving the trial to Jerusalem; Paul, a Roman citizen, appealed to Caesar to escape that trap, verses 9 to 11.

options.text: Because he had a right as a Roman citizen and stood before Caesar's judgment seat.
options.note: True, this is the legal ground of the appeal, but the immediate reason was to escape being sent to Jerusalem.

options.text: Because he wanted to see the city of Rome as a tourist.
options.note: Paul did long to reach Rome for the gospel, but the appeal was a legal move to avoid injustice, not sightseeing.

options.text: Because he admitted he was guilty and sought the emperor's mercy.
options.note: Paul insisted he was innocent; he did not confess guilt.

quiz.q: How does Festus sum up the real dispute when he explains it to Agrippa?

options.text: It concerns a certain Jesus who had died, whom Paul affirmed to be alive.
options.note: Festus reduces the whole case to the resurrection question, verse 19, the heart of the gospel.

options.text: It is a disagreement about the Jewish religion.
options.note: Festus does call it a matter of their own religion, but he pinpoints it further to the claim that Jesus is alive.

options.text: It is a clear case of sedition against Rome.
options.note: Festus says the opposite; the accusers did not charge Paul with the crimes he had expected.

options.text: It is a property dispute between Paul and the priests.
options.note: Nothing of the kind; the dispute is religious and centers on Jesus and the resurrection.

quiz.q: What did the Jerusalem leaders really want when they asked Festus to summon Paul?

options.text: To ambush and kill him on the road to Jerusalem.
options.note: Luke tells us plainly they were preparing an ambush to kill him along the way, verse 3.

options.text: To have the case tried in Jerusalem rather than Caesarea.
options.note: That was their stated request, but the hidden aim was murder by ambush.

options.text: To give Paul a fairer hearing among his own people.
options.note: The opposite of fairness; they sought his death, not justice.

options.text: To release him quietly to avoid trouble.
options.note: They wanted him dead, not released.

quiz.q: What was Festus's problem as he prepared to send Paul to Rome?

options.text: He had no clear charge to write against a prisoner he found innocent.
options.note: Festus admits he found nothing worthy of death and had nothing definite to write, verses 25 to 27.

options.text: He needed Agrippa's help to understand the Jewish dispute.
options.note: True, he consulted Agrippa, but the root problem was having no real charge to state.

options.text: He was unsure whether Paul was a Roman citizen.
options.note: Citizenship was not the issue; the appeal to Caesar had already been accepted.

options.text: He believed Paul was clearly guilty of a capital crime.
options.note: He believed the opposite; he found Paul had done nothing worthy of death.

quiz.q: How does this chapter show God's purpose at work?

options.text: The plot fails and the appeal sends Paul to Rome, just as the Lord had promised.
options.note: God had said Paul must testify in Rome, Acts 23:11; the events here carry that promise forward.

options.text: God protects Paul from the ambush by keeping him in Caesarea.
options.note: True in part, but the larger point is that the appeal advances God's plan to reach Rome, not merely safety.

options.text: God rewards Paul with immediate release for his patience.
options.note: Paul was not released; he remained a prisoner, yet God's purpose still advanced.

options.text: God's plan is frustrated by the schemes of the powerful.
options.note: The schemes fail; God's purpose to bring Paul to Rome moves steadily forward.

questions: Paul faced renewed danger with steady composure, resting in God's promise that he would reach Rome. Where do you need to trust that God's purpose for you will hold, even under pressure?
Paul used his lawful rights wisely without ever surrendering his trust in God. How do you hold together practical action and faith when facing an unfair situation?
Festus could name Jesus only as a dead man Paul claimed was alive. Who around you knows the name of Jesus but has never faced the question of the resurrection?
God turned a hostile plot and a legal appeal into the road to Rome. Can you look back and see God weaving even opposition into His good purpose for you?
Paul stood before kings and governors as a prisoner, yet kept testifying to Christ. Where has God placed you, however lowly it feels, to bear witness to Him?

translationNotes.note: I appeal to Caesar translates the Greek epikaloumai, a formal legal appeal by which a Roman citizen removed his case to the emperor's court. The same verb elsewhere means to call upon the Lord; the citizen appeals to Caesar, but Scripture points beyond Caesar to a higher throne.

The Emperor and, in verse 26, our sovereign render the Greek Sebastos, the august one, the title Augustus borne by the Roman emperor. Acts uses such titles knowingly, since it everywhere proclaims that Jesus, not Caesar, is Lord of all.

Religion translates a Greek word that can mean religion or superstition; Festus speaks as a puzzled outsider. What he cannot grasp, that a crucified Jesus is alive, is the very center of the Christian message.
